"Yawma" is an adverb of time, "saffan" is an absolute object, "man" is a nominative substitution from the subject of "speak," and "sawaban" is an accusative object qualifying an omitted noun.
78:38.translation_en: The Day when the Spirit and the angels stand in ranks, they will not speak except for one whom the Most Merciful permits, and who says what is right.
78:38.transliteration: Yawma yaqumu ar-ruhu wal-mala'ikatu saffan la yatakallamuna illa man adhina lahu ar-Rahmanu waqala sawaba

words_en.note_en: An accusative adverb of time linked to the earlier negated verb "yamlikuna," meaning "on the Day."
A present-tense verb meaning "stands," forming a clause that defines the adverb "Day."
The nominative subject of "stands," referring to the Spirit.
Joined by "and" to "the Spirit," sharing the nominative case as part of the subject.
An accusative absolute object of an omitted verb, conveying that they stand arrayed in ranks.
A particle of negation giving the following verb a negated sense.
A present-tense verb meaning "they speak," with its embedded plural subject.
A particle of exception introducing what is excluded from the negation.
A relative pronoun in the nominative position, standing as a substitute from the subject of "they speak."
A past-tense verb meaning "permitted," forming the relative clause of "man."
It attaches to the verb "permitted," meaning "for him."
The nominative subject of "permitted," naming the Most Merciful.
A past-tense verb "and says/said," joined to "permitted" within the relative clause.
The accusative object qualifying an omitted noun, the sense being "says correct speech."

78:38.irab_en: "Yawma" is an accusative adverb of time tied to the negated verb "yamlikuna" of the previous verse. Its clause is formed by "yaqumu" (stands), whose nominative subject is "al-ruh," with "al-mala'ika" conjoined to it and likewise nominative. "Saffan" is an accusative absolute object of an implied verb, picturing them ranged in ranks. The next clause begins with the negation "la" and the verb "yatakallamuna" with its plural subject. "Illa" introduces an exception, and "man" is a nominative relative pronoun functioning as a substitute (badal) from the subject of "speak." Its clause is "adhina lahu al-Rahman," with "al-Rahman" the nominative subject. "Wa qala" is conjoined within that clause, and "sawaban" is its accusative object, qualifying an omitted noun, meaning "correct speech."

faqs_en.q: Why is "yawma" in the accusative case?
faqs_en.a: It is an adverb of time (zarf zaman) connected to the negated verb "yamlikuna" from the previous verse, so it carries the accusative case.

faqs_en.q: What is the grammatical role of "man" in this verse?
faqs_en.a: It is a relative pronoun in the nominative position acting as a substitute (badal) from the subject of the verb "they speak."

faqs_en.q: Why is "saffan" accusative?
faqs_en.a: It is an absolute object (maf'ul mutlaq) of an omitted verb, describing how they stand arranged in ranks.

78:38.uthmani: يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلرُّوحُ وَٱلْمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ صَفًّۭا ۖ لَّا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنُ وَقَالَ صَوَابًۭا

78:39.h1_en: I'rab of Surah An-Naba Ayah 39 — word-by-word Arabic grammar
78:39.tldr_en: This ayah declares that Day to be the True Day, and whoever wills may take a path back to his Lord. "Al-yawm" is a nominative substitute for the demonstrative, "man" is a conditional pronoun functioning as subject, "sha'a" is the condition verb, and "ila rabbihi" relates to the omitted object of "takes."
78:39.translation_en: That is the True Day; so whoever wills may take a way back to his Lord.
78:39.transliteration: Dhalika al-yawmu al-haqqu faman sha'a ittakhadha ila rabbihi ma'aba

words_en.note_en: A demonstrative pronoun in the nominative position serving as the subject, meaning "that."
A nominative substitute for the demonstrative, or an explanatory apposition to it.
The nominative predicate completing the sentence, meaning "the True (Day)."
"Fa" links to an implied conditional answer, and "man" is a conditional pronoun in the nominative position as subject.
A past-tense verb in the jussive position as the verb of the condition, its subject referring back to "man."
A past-tense verb in the jussive position serving as the answer of the condition, meaning "takes/took."
A preposition meaning "to/toward," governing the following noun in the genitive.
It is governed by "to" in the genitive; the phrase relates to a circumstantial state (hal) of "ma'aba," the second object of "takes."
The accusative second object of "takes," meaning "a return/refuge," the first object being omitted (i.e., faith).

78:39.irab_en: The verse begins with the demonstrative "dhalika" as the subject (mubtada'). "Al-yawm" follows as a nominative substitute (badal) for the demonstrative, or as an explanatory apposition, and "al-haqq" is the nominative predicate. The second sentence opens with "fa," which links to an implied conditional structure, and "man" is a conditional pronoun in the nominative position serving as subject. "Sha'a" is a past-tense verb in the jussive position as the condition's verb, its subject referring to "man." "Ittakhadha" is likewise in the jussive position as the answer of the condition. "Ila rabbihi" is genitive and relates to a circumstantial state of "ma'aba," which is the accusative second object of "takes"; its first object is omitted, the implied sense being "takes faith as a way back to his Lord."

faqs_en.q: Why is "al-yawm" in the nominative case?
faqs_en.a: It is a substitute (badal) for the nominative demonstrative "dhalika," or an explanatory apposition to it, so it shares the nominative case.

faqs_en.q: What is the function of "man" after "fa"?
faqs_en.a: It is a conditional pronoun in the nominative position acting as the subject (mubtada'), with "sha'a" as its condition and "ittakhadha" as the answer.

faqs_en.q: Why is "ma'aba" accusative?
faqs_en.a: It is the second object (maf'ul thani) of "ittakhadha," while the first object is omitted, giving the sense "took [faith] as a return to his Lord."

78:39.uthmani: ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ مَـَٔابًا

78:40.h1_en: I'rab of Surah An-Naba Ayah 40 — word-by-word Arabic grammar
78:40.tldr_en: This concluding ayah warns of a near punishment, on a Day when each person sees what his hands have sent forth, and the disbeliever cries out wishing he were dust. "Adhaban" is the second object of "warned," "yawma" is an adverb of time, "ma" is the object of "sees," and "ya" is a particle of alerting.
78:40.translation_en: Indeed, We have warned you of a near punishment, on the Day a person will see what his hands have sent ahead, and the disbeliever will say, "O, I wish I were dust!"
78:40.transliteration: Inna andharnakum 'adhaban qariban yawma yanzuru al-mar'u ma qaddamat yadahu wayaqulu al-kafiru ya laytani kuntu turaba

words_en.role_en: emphatic particle with noun (inna wa ismuha)
words_en.note_en: The particle "inna" with its attached pronoun "we" as its noun, giving emphasis to the statement.

words_en.role_en: verb, subject and first object (fi'l, fa'il, maf'ul)
words_en.note_en: A past-tense verb "we warned" with the suffixed "you" as its first object; the clause is the predicate of "inna."
The accusative second object of "warned," meaning "a punishment."
An adjective describing "punishment," agreeing in the accusative case, meaning "near."
An accusative adverb of time linked to "punishment," meaning "on the Day."
A present-tense verb "sees/looks," here carrying the sense of perceiving, forming the clause of the adverb "Day."
The nominative subject of "sees," meaning "the person."
A relative pronoun in the accusative position as the object of "sees," since the verb carries the sense of "perceives."
A past-tense verb "sent forth/put forward," forming the relative clause of "ma."
The nominative subject of "sent forth," a dual noun meaning "his two hands."
A present-tense verb "and says," joined by "and" to the preceding clause.
The nominative subject of "says," meaning "the disbeliever."

words_en.role_en: particle of alerting with wishing particle (ya wa layta)
words_en.note_en: "Ya" is a particle of alerting, and "layta" expresses a wish, with the attached "me" as its noun.

words_en.role_en: defective verb with subject (kana wa ismuha)
words_en.note_en: The verb "I was" with its attached subject pronoun, functioning as the predicate of "layta."
The accusative predicate of "I was," meaning "dust," expressing the wish to be mere dust.

78:40.irab_en: The verse opens with "inna" and its attached pronoun "we" as its noun. The clause "andharnakum" (we warned you) is its predicate, the suffixed "you" being the first object. "Adhaban" is the accusative second object, qualified by the adjective "qariban" (near), also accusative. "Yawma" is an accusative adverb of time tied to "punishment," and its clause is "yanzuru al-mar'u," with "al-mar'u" the nominative subject; the verb carries the sense of perceiving, so "ma" is a relative pronoun in the accusative position as its object. "Qaddamat" forms the relative clause, with the dual "yadahu" (his two hands) as its nominative subject. "Wa yaqulu al-kafiru" is conjoined, "al-kafir" being its subject. "Ya" is a particle of alerting, "layta" a wishing particle with attached "me," "kuntu" the defective verb "I was," and "turaban" its accusative predicate.

faqs_en.q: Why is "ma" in the accusative position in this verse?
faqs_en.a: Because the verb "yanzuru" here carries the meaning of "perceives/sees," so "ma" functions as its direct object (maf'ul bihi) in the accusative position.

faqs_en.q: What is the grammatical role of "yadahu"?
faqs_en.a: It is the nominative subject (fa'il) of the verb "qaddamat," and being a dual noun it is marked by the alif, meaning "his two hands."

faqs_en.q: Why is "turaban" in the accusative case?
faqs_en.a: It is the predicate (khabar) of the defective verb "kuntu" (I was), which puts its predicate in the accusative, expressing the wish "I were dust."

78:40.uthmani: إِنَّآ أَنذَرْنَٰكُمْ عَذَابًۭا قَرِيبًۭا يَوْمَ يَنظُرُ ٱلْمَرْءُ مَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاهُ وَيَقُولُ ٱلْكَافِرُ يَٰلَيْتَنِى كُنتُ تُرَٰبًۢا
